revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:符天暦]]
{{Infobox Calendar | title = Futian Calendar | pic = | pic_caption = | type = [[Lunisolar calendar]] | introduced = {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|qid=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}}, during the [[Jianzhong era]] (780–783) | inventor = [[Cao Shili]] (曹士蔿) | accuracy = 1 tropical year ≈ 365.2448 days | basis = {{ill|Chinese calendar|en|Chinese calendar|qid=Q134032|lt=Chinese calendar|1=Chinese calendar}}, {{ill|Indian astronomy|en|Indian astronomy|qid=Q1292962|lt=Indian astronomy|1=Indian astronomy}} | related = [[Three Concordant Calendar]], [[Shou Shi Calendar]], [[Senmyō Calendar]] }}
The Futian Calendar ({{zh|s=符天历|t=符天曆|p=Fútiān lì}}, Japanese: 符天暦, *Futenreki*) was a Chinese lunisolar calendar compiled during the Jianzhong era (780–783) of the Tang dynasty by Cao Shili (曹士蔿). Although it was never officially adopted as the state calendar, its calculation methods had a lasting influence on later Chinese calendars. It was widely used for astrological purposes in both China and Japan.
== Overview == The Futian Calendar rejected the Shangyuan Jinian method (上元积年法), which had been used since the Three Concordant Calendar (三统暦). Instead, it employed the proximate epoch method, setting its epoch at the solar term "Rain Water" (雨水) in 660 CE (the 5th year of Emperor Gaozong’s Xianqing era).<ref name="Futian780">Tang Calendar Research, 2005</ref>
The calendar introduced a decimal fraction system called "ten-thousandths division" (*wanfenfa*, 万分法), using 1 year 365.2448 days (approximately matching the modern tropical year). This system influenced later calendars, such as: * Zhaoyuan Calendar (調元暦) of the Later Jin dynasty * Shou Shi Calendar (授時暦) of the Yuan dynasty
Additionally, its mean solar anomaly calculations influenced the Chongxuan Calendar (崇玄暦) in the late Tang dynasty.<ref name="Futian780"/>
== Influence of Indian Astronomy == The Futian Calendar incorporated elements of Indian astronomy, such as calculations for the Nine Luminaries (*Jiuyou*, 九曜), including: * Sun (日) * Moon (月) * Five Classical Planets (五星) * Rahu (羅睺) * Ketu (計都)
These elements linked the calendar to Chinese astrology, leading to its widespread use in divination and astrology.<ref name="Astrology2000">Chinese Astrology in the Tang Dynasty, 2000</ref>
---
== Adoption and Usage in Japan == The Futian Calendar was introduced to Japan by the Tendai monk Nichien (日延), where it became associated with Tendai Esoteric Buddhism and the Shukuyōdō system (宿曜道), a Buddhist-influenced divination method.<ref name="Nichien950">Japanese Buddhist Calendars, 2012</ref>
According to Ichijō Kanera (一条兼良) during the Muromachi period, the Futian Calendar was briefly adopted in 958 CE (2nd year of the Tentoku era), becoming an alternative to the official Senmyō Calendar (宣明暦).<ref group="note">However, the original source for this claim is lost and known only through '''Tanigawa Shikisei’s''' *Nihon Shoki Tōshō*.</ref> While no official records confirm this, historical texts such as the Kuyōki (小右記) indicate that both the Senmyō and Futian Calendars were studied together.<ref name="Kanera1450">Kanera’s Calendar Theories, 1450</ref>
Further evidence suggests that the Futian Calendar influenced calendar reforms. A 1240 CE Guchūreki (annotated calendar) found among the Maeda Collection (前田育英会) contains Futian Calendar formulas for a lunar eclipse prediction on October 16 of that year. This indicates that, while never officially adopted, the calendar was used for adjusting and verifying other calendars.<ref name="Maeda1240">Japanese Astronomical Records, 2001</ref>
---
